title: Binomial Tree and Black-Scholes-Merton Test Cases
text:
CPP Pricing Engine Alejandro Naranjo Caraza 2026-03-01

Overview
In order to vertify the general functionality of the option pricing engine, the following code runs basic test cases for european and american options. It also tests edge cases, such as options with unerlying determinstic stock (zero volatility) and no stock yield (zero risk free interest rate). Examples of options with continuous dividend yield and known dividend cash schedule are also tested.
Each example compares prices obtained through Black-Scholes-Merton (BSM) model and through the binomial model for both call and put options. Convergence of the binomial model is also analyzed by plotting calculated price (y-axis) against the number of nodes used (x-axis).
Note that the option pricing models (BSM as well as binomial) depend on the following variables:

T: Option time to maturity
K: Option strike price
Option Type: Call vs put option
Exercise Type: American vs european
S0: Current price of underlying asset
r: Risk free annual rate
q: Continuous dividend yield
sigma: Stock volatility

All of the following test cases use the Cox-Ross-Rubinstein parameterization for u, d, a in the binomial model.


Load Libraries

import math
import sys
sys.path.append('../cpp_pricing/build')
import pricer
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt



Global Functions
The following functions are used throughout this section to build stock, option and model objects from pricing parameters, as well as calculate the prices and plot results from the binomial model against the number of iterations used.

# Takes option and market parameters and prints out vanilla option price (Binomial Tree + BSM).
def getPrice(
    T,K,optType,exeType,
    S0,r,q,sigma,
    return_ = False,
    n=1000
):
    # Build option
    
    vanillaOption = pricer.makeVanillaOption(K,T,optType,exeType)
    
    # Bulild stock
    
    stock = pricer.makeStock(S0,q)    
    
    if exeType == 'e':

        # Build Binomial Model
            
        binomial = pricer.makeBinomial(vanillaOption,stock,r,sigma,n)
        
        # Build Black-Scholes-Metron model

        bsm = pricer.makeBSM(vanillaOption,stock,r,sigma)

        # Get vanilla option price through binomial tree (backwards induction)

        binomialPrice = binomial.getPrice()
        print("Price (Binomial): ",binomialPrice)

        # Get vanilla option price from Black-Scholes-Merton formula

        bsmPrice = bsm.getPrice()
        print("Price (BSM): ",bsmPrice)
    
    else:
        
        # Build Binomial Model
            
        binomial = pricer.makeBinomial(vanillaOption,stock,r,sigma,n)
        
        # Build Black-Scholes-Metron model
        
        european = pricer.makeVanillaOption(K,T,optType,'e')
        bsm = pricer.makeBSM(european,stock,r,sigma)
        
        # Get vanilla option price through binomial tree (backwards induction)

        binomialPrice = binomial.getPrice()
        print("Price (Binomial): ",binomialPrice)

        # Get vanilla option price from Black-Scholes-Merton formula

        bsmPrice = bsm.getPrice()
        print("Price (BSM): ",bsmPrice)
    
    if return_:
        
        return binomialPrice,bsmPrice
        
# Takes option and market parameters. Plots binomial tree price convergence as #iterations increase.
def plotBinomial(
    T,K,optType,exeType,
    S0,r,q,sigma,
):
    # Build option
    
    vanillaOption = pricer.makeVanillaOption(K,T,optType,exeType)
    
    # Bulild stock
    
    stock = pricer.makeStock(S0,q)
    
    numIters = []
    prices = []
    
    for n in range (1,26):
        numIters.append(n*20)
        binomial = pricer.makeBinomial(vanillaOption,stock,r,sigma,n*20)
        prices.append(binomial.getPrice())
    
    plt.plot(numIters,prices)
    plt.xlabel("Iterations / Time steps")
    plt.ylabel("Price")
    plt.title("Binomial Tree Convergence with Increase in Iterations")
    plt.show()



Example 1: Baseline
This is a traditional benchmark example (used in Hull, Options, Futures and Other Derivatives). Notice Binomial and BSM prices converge as we increase the number of nodes in the Binomial Model.

T = 1
K = 100
optType = 'c'
exeType = 'e'

S0 = 100
r = 0.05
q = 0

sigma = 0.2


getPrice(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)

Price (Binomial):  10.448584103764581
Price (BSM):  10.450583572185565



plotBinomial(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)









optType = 'p'


getPrice(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)
plotBinomial(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)

Price (Binomial):  5.57152655383364
Price (BSM):  5.573526022256971











Example 2: Put-Call Parity
Test case to check put-call parity: 
C - P = S e^{-qT} - K e^{-rT}


T = 2
K = 100
optType = 'c'
exeType = 'e'

S0 = 120
r = 0.03
q = 0

sigma = 0.25


binomialCallPrice, bsmCallPrice = getPrice(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma,True)
plotBinomial(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)

Price (Binomial):  31.279687624099125
Price (BSM):  31.279802719901866










optType = 'p'


binomialPutPrice, bsmPutPrice = getPrice(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma,True)
plotBinomial(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)

Price (Binomial):  5.456140982516954
Price (BSM):  5.456256078326742









Notice put-call parity is satisfied:

print(binomialCallPrice - binomialPutPrice)
print(bsmCallPrice - bsmPutPrice)
print(S0 - K * math.exp(-r*T))

25.823546641582173
25.823546641575124
25.823546641575135




Example 3: Deep ITM Option
We consider a call option where strike price is well below the current underlying stock’s price.(Notice these options are generally more expensive because their strike price is favorable for current market conditions.)

T = 1
K = 100
optType = 'c'
exeType = 'e'

S0 = 200
r = 0.05
q = 0

sigma = 0.2


getPrice(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)
plotBinomial(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)

Price (Binomial):  104.8777156117045
Price (BSM):  104.87772423432371









Notice that the put option equivalent (OTM) will be valued at a much lower price.

optType = 'p'


getPrice(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)
plotBinomial(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)

Price (Binomial):  0.0006580617751369543
Price (BSM):  0.0006666843951132258











Example 4. Deep OTM Option
We consider the opposite case, where a call option’s strike price is well above the current underlying stock’s price.

T = 1
K = 100
optType = 'c'
exeType = 'e'

S0 = 50
r = 0.05
q = 0

sigma = 0.2


getPrice(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)
plotBinomial(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)

Price (Binomial):  0.002366016380139772
Price (BSM):  0.002399417553309663









Notice that the put option equivalent (ITM) will be valued at a much higher price.

optType = 'p'


getPrice(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)
plotBinomial(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)

Price (Binomial):  45.12530846644839
Price (BSM):  45.12534186762472











5. Example: Zero Volatility
We consider a zero volatility (sigma=0) edge case. Notice that the binomial tree collapses into a fully deterministic system.
If the option is european, the price is the present value of the option’s payoff, given that the underlying stock exhibits constant exponential growth (annual yield equivalent to risk free rate minus continuous dividend yield).
On the other hand, an american call option where the risk free rate is larger than the dividend yield will be exercised at maturity and its price is equivalent to the european option.
An american call option where the risk free rate is smaller than the dividend yield will be exercised immediately and the option’s price is the payoff at t=0.
An american put option will also be exercised immediately and its price is equivalent to the option’s payoff at t=0.

T = 1
K = 100
optType = 'c'
exeType = 'e'

S0 = 100
r = 0.05
q = 0

sigma = 0.0


getPrice(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)
plotBinomial(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)

Price (Binomial):  4.877057549928611
Price (BSM):  4.877057549928594










optType = 'p'


getPrice(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)
plotBinomial(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)

Price (Binomial):  0.0
Price (BSM):  0.0











6. Example: Zero Time to Maturity
We consider a zero time to maturity (T=0) edge case.Notice that the option’s price is its immediate payoff.

T = 0
K = 100
optType = 'c'
exeType = 'e'

S0 = 105
r = 0.05
q = 0.01

sigma = 0.2


getPrice(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)
plotBinomial(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)

Price (Binomial):  5.0
Price (BSM):  5.0










optType = 'p'


getPrice(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)
plotBinomial(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)

Price (Binomial):  0.0
Price (BSM):  0.0











7. Example: Zero Interest Rate
Consider zero interest rate edge case. Notice that models are still non-determinstic but without time value of money (future cash flows are worth the same as today’s). Discounting disappears in both models.

T = 1
K = 100
optType = 'c'
exeType = 'e'

S0 = 100
r = 0
q = 0

sigma = 0.2


getPrice(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)
plotBinomial(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)

Price (Binomial):  7.9635763260249135
Price (BSM):  7.965567455405804










optType = 'p'


getPrice(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)
plotBinomial(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)

Price (Binomial):  7.963576326024925
Price (BSM):  7.965567455405804











8. Example: High Volatility
High volatility test for numerical stability.

T = 1
K = 100
optType = 'c'
exeType = 'e'

S0 = 100
r = 0.05
q = 0

sigma = 1.0


getPrice(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)
plotBinomial(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)

Price (Binomial):  39.830854144987555
Price (BSM):  39.84016248343717










optType = 'p'


getPrice(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)
plotBinomial(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)

Price (Binomial):  34.953796595056566
Price (BSM):  34.963104933508575











9. Example: American Option
With american options, the option can be exercised at or before maturity.
Notice that, in the case of a call option on a non-dividend paying asset, it will never be optimal to exercise before maturity, as holding the option preserves time value. Its price is equivalent to that of a european option.
In the case of an american put, it can be optimal to exercise before maturity, specially when the option is deep ITM. This means that the american put can often be more valuable than its european equivalent.

T = 1
K = 100
optType = 'c'
exeType = 'a'

S0 = 100
r = 0.05
q = 0

sigma = 0.2


getPrice(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)
plotBinomial(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)

Price (Binomial):  10.448584103764581
Price (BSM):  10.450583572185565










optType = 'p'


getPrice(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)
plotBinomial(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)

Price (Binomial):  6.089595282977866
Price (BSM):  5.573526022256971











10. Example: American Option

T = 1
K = 40
optType = 'p'
exeType = 'a'

S0 = 40
r = 0.06
q = 0

sigma = 0.2


getPrice(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)
plotBinomial(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)

Price (Binomial):  2.319278261909931
Price (BSM):  2.0664010044203422










optType = 'c'


getPrice(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)
plotBinomial(T,K,optType,exeType,S0,r,q,sigma)

Price (Binomial):  4.395014477558624
Price (BSM):  4.395819661050393











11. Example: Binomial with Known Dividend Cash Schedule
For a european option with a known dividend schedule, expected dividend payments reduce the future price of the underlying asset. As a result, put options become more valuable relative to the non-dividend case, while call options become less valuable.

divCash = [
    [3.5/12,2.06]
]

T = 5 / 12
K = 50
optType = 'p'
exeType = 'a'

S0 = 52
r = 0.1
q = 0

sigma = 0.4
n = 1000


# Build option

american = pricer.makeVanillaOption(K,T,optType,exeType)
european = pricer.makeVanillaOption(K,T,optType,'e')

# Bulild stock

stock = pricer.makeStock(S0,q,divCash)

# Build binomial model

binomial = pricer.makeBinomial(american,stock,r,sigma,n)

# Build Black-Scholes-Metron model

bsm = pricer.makeBSM(european,stock,r,sigma)


# Get vanilla option price through binomial tree (backwards induction)

binomialPut = binomial.getPrice()
print("Option price with dividend cash schedule (binomial): ",binomialPut)

# Get vanilla option price from Black-Scholes-Merton formula

print("Option price without dividend cash schedule (BSM): ",bsm.getPrice())

Option price with dividend cash schedule (binomial):  4.219995934142615
Option price without dividend cash schedule (BSM):  3.362040105114886



optType = 'c'
n = 1000


# Build option

american = pricer.makeVanillaOption(K,T,optType,exeType)
european = pricer.makeVanillaOption(K,T,optType,'e')

# Bulild stock

stock = pricer.makeStock(S0,q,divCash)

# Build binomial model

binomial = pricer.makeBinomial(american,stock,r,sigma,n)

# Build Black-Scholes-Metron model

bsm = pricer.makeBSM(european,stock,r,sigma)


# Get vanilla option price through binomial tree (backwards induction)

binomialCall = binomial.getPrice()
print("Option price with dividend cash schedule (binomial): ",binomialCall)

# Get vanilla option price from Black-Scholes-Merton formula

print("Option price without dividend cash schedule (BSM): ",bsm.getPrice())

Option price with dividend cash schedule (binomial):  6.510884204925204
Option price without dividend cash schedule (BSM):  7.402567249657977
crumbs: Test Cases for BSM and Binomial C++ Pricing Library

title: Stock Price Behaviour and Assumptions Under Black-Scholes-Merton and Binomial Tree
text:
CPP Pricing Engine Alejandro Naranjo Caraza 2026-03-01

Overview
The following presents the basic assumptions regarding stock price behaviour in option valuation. The Black-Scholes-Merton (BSM) model and the Binomial Tree model propose similar ideas regarding stock price fluctuations, but, while BSM is based on a continuous process (a Geometric Brownian Motion, specifically), the Binomial Model follows a discrete-time, discrete-state random walk.



Load Libraries

import sys

sys.path.append('../cpp_pricing/build')
sys.path.append('../cpp_stocksim/build')

import pricer
import stocksim

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import random
import math




Relevant Models
Stochastic Process
A stochastic process is a family of random variables

\{X(t) : t \in T\}

defined on a probability space, where the index set T typically represents time.
A stochastic process is called a continuous-time, continuous-state stochastic process if the index set T is continuous (e.g., T \subseteq \mathbb{R}) and each random variable X(t) takes values in a continuous state space (e.g., \mathbb{R}).
Random Walk
A discrete-time random walk can be defined as a stochastic process \{S_n : n=0,1,2,..\} where S_n is the sum of independant and identically distributed (i.i.d.) random variables (steps) X_i: 
S_n = S_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n}X_i

Alternatively, a multiplicative version can be defined as a process \{V_n : n=0,1,2,..\} where V_n is the product of i.i.d. random variables: 
V_n = V_0 \prod_{i=1}^{n}X_i

Note the the next position depends only on the previous one (Markov property): 
\begin{align*}
S_{n+1} &= S_n + X_{n+1}\\
V_{n+1} &= V_n X_{n+1}
\end{align*}

Wiener Process / Brownian Motion
Real-valued continuous-time stochastic process \{W_t\}_{t \ge 0} with the following properties:

W_0 = 0 almost surely.
W has independent increments. For every t > 0, the future increments W_{t+u}-W_t,\quad u\ge 0, are independent of the past values W_s, s < t.
W has Gaussian increments. For all u,t \ge 0, W_{t+u} - W_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,u). That is, a time step u results in an increment that is normally distributed with mean 0 and variance u.
W has almost surely continuous paths: W_t is almost surely continuous in t.

Generalized Weiner Process
A Generalized Weiner Process for a variable x can be defined 
dx = a dt + b dz
 where - z follows a Weiner Process - a and b are constants - The mean change per unit change in time (drift rate) is a - The variance per unit time is (variance rate) is b²
Ito’s Process
Generalization of Generalized Weiner Process: 
dx = a(x,t) dt + b(x,t) dz
 where a=a(x,t) and b=b(x,t) are deterministic function of (x,t).
Geometric Brownian Motion
A stochastic process for a variable x follows a Geometric Brownian Motion if it satisfies the following stochastic differential equation (SDE):

dx = \mu x dt + \sigma x dz
 where z follows a Weiner Process and \mu, \sigma are constants. Notice that the left side of the equation models a determinstic trend while the right side models uncertainty.



Stock Price Behaviour and Assumptions in Black-Scholes-Merton Model
Under BSM, stock price follows a Geometric Brownian Motion:

\begin{align*}
dS &= \mu S dt + \sigma S dz \\
\dfrac{dS}{S} &= \mu dt + \sigma dz
\end{align*}

Where

S is the stock price
t is the time variable (usually in years)
z follows a standard Wiener process with drift rate 0 and variance rate 1
\mu is the stock’s expected return
\sigma is stock return volatility (\sigma² is the variance rate of the retun’s process)

Notice that, under this model, stock return follows a Generalized Weiner Process (dS = a dt + b dz). This is because investor’s expected return is independent of the stock’s price, meaning that constant expected price drift must be replaced by constant return. Considering dz = 0 and integrating between time 0 and T yields $S_T = S_0 e^{T} $.
Also notice that BSM assumes consistent return uncertainty, independant of stock price, for periods of time of the same length (dt).
The discrete-time version of the model is: 
\begin{align*}
\Delta S &= \mu S \Delta t + \sigma S \epsilon \sqrt{\Delta t} \\
\dfrac{\Delta S}{S} &= \mu \Delta t + \sigma \epsilon \sqrt{\Delta t}
\end{align*}
 where \epsilon follows a standard normal distribution.
\Delta S / S is approximately normal: \Delta S / S \approx N (\mu \Delta t, \sigma² \Delta t)
It can be shown that the log return is exactly normal: \ln(\dfrac{S + \Delta S}{S}) \sim N ((\mu - \dfrac{1}{2}\sigma²)\Delta t, \sigma² \Delta t)



Stock Price Simulation with Geometric Brownian Motion (GBM)
The Euler-Maruyama method provides the following numerical approximation to the Geometric Brownian Motion SDE seen above:

S_{t+\Delta t} = S_t (1 + r \Delta t + \sigma \epsilon_t \sqrt{\Delta t})
 where \epsilon_t \sim N(0,1).
However, an analytical solution can be obtained through Ito’s formula. Stock price simulation through this solution is preferred over Euler’s approximation.

S_t = S_0 \exp((\mu - \dfrac{\sigma²}{2})t + \sigma z_t)
 where S_0 = S(0) is the initial condition and z is the underlying Wiener Process or, in the discretized case 
S_{t+\Delta t} = S_t \exp((\mu - \dfrac{\sigma²}{2}) \Delta t + \sigma \epsilon_t \sqrt{\Delta t})
 where \epsilon_t \sim N(0,1).
Stock Simulation Thorugh GBM (Euler-Maruyama) | Pseudocode
Given
    S0      Initial stock price
    dt      Time step size
    r       Risk-free rate
    sigma   Volatility
    T       Number of time steps

Initialize
    S[0] ← S0

For t ← 0 to T-1 do

    eps <- Sample from Normal(0,1)
    S[t+1] <- S[t] * (1 + r * dt + sigma * eps * sqrt(dt))

End For
Stock Simulation Thorugh GBM (Exact Solution) | Pseudocode
Given
    S0      Initial stock price
    dt      Time step size
    r       Risk-free rate
    sigma   Volatility
    T       Number of time steps

Initialize
    S[0] ← S0

For t ← 0 to T-1 do

    eps <- Sample from Normal(0,1)
    S[t+1] <- S[t] * exp[(r - sigma²/2) * dt + sigma * eps * sqrt(dt)]

End For
Stock Simulation Through GBM | Python Implementation

def stockGbmEuler(
    S0,
    T,
    r,
    sigma,
    n_paths = 1,
    n_steps = 100,
    seed = None
):
    if seed is not None:
        np.random.seed(seed)
        
    dt = T/n_steps
    sqrt_dt = np.sqrt(dt)
    eps = np.random.normal(loc=0,scale=1,size=(n_paths,n_steps))
    prices = np.zeros((n_paths,n_steps+1))
    prices[:,0] = S0
    prices[:,1:] = S0 * np.cumprod(1 + r*dt + sigma*eps*sqrt_dt,axis=1)
    return prices

def stockGbmExact(
    S0,
    T,
    r,
    sigma,
    n_paths = 1,
    n_steps = 100,
    seed = None
):
    if seed is not None:
        np.random.seed(seed)
    
    dt = T/n_steps
    sqrt_dt = np.sqrt(dt)
    eps = np.random.normal(loc=0,scale=1,size=(n_paths,n_steps))
    prices = np.zeros((n_paths,n_steps + 1))
    prices[:,0] = S0
    prices[:,1:] = S0 * np.cumprod(np.exp((r-sigma**2/2)*dt + (sigma*sqrt_dt*eps)),axis=1)
    return prices


S0 = 50
T = 10
r = 0.1
sigma = 0.2
n_paths = 10
n_steps = 100

gbmEuler = stockGbmEuler(S0,T,r,sigma,n_paths,n_steps)
gbmExact = stockGbmExact(S0,T,r,sigma,n_paths,n_steps)

time = np.linspace(0, T, n_steps + 1)

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 5))
plt.plot(time, gbmEuler.T)
plt.title("Stock Simulation with GBM - Euler Approximation")
plt.xlabel("Time")
plt.ylabel("Stock Price")
plt.show()

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 5))
plt.plot(time, gbmExact.T)
plt.title("Stock Simulation with GBM - Exact Solution")
plt.xlabel("Time")
plt.ylabel("Stock Price")

plt.show()















Using our C++ implementation of the random walk process: ( Source code: geometricBrownian.cpp, geometricBrownian.h. )

gbmExactCPP = stocksim.geometricBrownian(
    S0,
    T,
    r,
    sigma,
    n_paths,
    n_steps
)

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 5))

for path in gbmExactCPP:
    plt.plot(time,path)
plt.title("Stock Simulation with GBM - Exact Solution\nC++ Implementation")
plt.xlabel("Time")
plt.ylabel("Stock Price")
plt.show()











Stock Price Behaviour Under Binomial Model
Under the Binomial Model, stock price follows a discrete-time discrete-spce multiplicative random walk. At each time step, the stock price S_n can either move up to S_{n+1}=u S_n or down to S_{n+1}=d S_n (with d<1, u>1). The probability of a price increase is p, while the probability of a decrease is 1-p.
This results in the following expected price at each time step: E[S_{n+1}] = p (u S_n) + (1-p) (d S_n). Under the no-arbitrage assumption in the binomial model, the probability p in a risk-neutral framework is determined by p = \dfrac{e^{rT}-d}{u-d} where r is the risk-free rate.
John C. Cox, Stephen A. Ross, and Mark Rubinstein (CRR) Parameterization
The following parameterization is commonly adopted because it matches the volatility of the underlying asset and guarantees convergence of the binomial model to the Black–Scholes–Merton framework as \Delta t tends to zero. 
\begin{align*}
p &= \dfrac{a-d}{u-d}\\
u &= e^{\sigma\sqrt{\Delta t}}\\
d &= e^{-\sigma\sqrt{\Delta t}}\\
a &= e^{r\Delta t}
\end{align*}

where

r is the risk-free rate
\sigma is the stock return volatility
a is the discount rate

Stock Simulation with Binomial Model | Pseudocode
Given
    S0      Initial stock price
    dt      Time step size
    r       Risk-free rate
    sigma   Volatility
    T       Number of time steps

Compute
    u ← exp(sigma * sqrt(dt))
    d ← 1 / u
    a ← exp(r * dt)
    p ← (a - d) / (u - d)

Initialize
    S[0] ← S0

For t ← 0 to T-1 do

    Simulate X ~ Bernoulli(p)

    If X = 1 then
        S[t+1] ← S[t] * u
    Else
        S[t+1] ← S[t] * d

End For
Stock Simulation Through Binomial Model | Python Implementation
The following is a simple simulation of stock price behaviour with a multiplicative random walk (CRR parameterization).

def stockRandomWalk(
    S0,
    T,
    r,
    sigma,
    n_paths = 1,
    n_steps = 100,
    seed = None
):
    if seed is not None:
        np.random.seed(seed)
        
    dt = T/n_steps
    u = np.exp(sigma * np.sqrt(dt))
    d = 1/u
    a = np.exp(r*dt)
    p = (a-d) / (u-d)
    
    bernoulli = np.random.binomial(n=1,p=p,size=(n_paths,n_steps))
    moves = bernoulli * u + (1 - bernoulli) * d
    
    prices = np.empty((n_paths,n_steps + 1))
    prices[:,0] = S0
    prices[:,1:] = S0 * np.cumprod(moves,axis=1)

    return prices


S0 = 50
T = 10
r = 0.1
sigma = 0.2
n_paths = 10
n_steps = 100

binomial = stockRandomWalk(S0,T,r,sigma,n_paths,n_steps)


time = np.linspace(0, T, n_steps + 1)

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 5))
plt.plot(time, binomial.T)
plt.title("Stock Simulation with Random Walk")
plt.xlabel("Time")
plt.ylabel("Stock Price")

plt.show()








Using our C++ implementation of the random walk process: ( Source code: randomWalk.cpp, randomWalk.h. )

binomialCPP = stocksim.randomWalk(S0,T,r,sigma,n_paths,n_steps)

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 5))

for path in binomialCPP:
    plt.plot(time,path)
plt.title("Stock Simulation with Random Walk\nC++ Implementation")
plt.xlabel("Time")
plt.ylabel("Stock Price")
plt.show()
crumbs: Stock Price Behaviour and Assumptions Under BSM and Binomial Models

title: Financial Pricing Basics | Implementation in C++ and Python
text:
CPP Pricing Engine Alejandro Naranjo Caraza 2026-03-01
This repository provides an introduction to the fundamental pricing models used in modern finance. It covers frameworks such as the Black–Scholes–Merton option pricing model and the binomial tree model, as well as an overview of forwards and futures. The repository aims to document the theoretical foundations of these models along with basic implementations in C++ and Python.
The content is based on the following books:

Hull, John. Options, Futures, and Other Derivatives. Pearson, 2022.
Taylor, Stephen. Modelling Financial Time Series. World Scientific, 2013.


Index

Stock Price Behaviour and Assumptions Under Black-Scholes-Merton and Binomial Tree
Binomial Tree and Black-Scholes-Merton Overview and Implementation
Binomial Tree and Black-Scholes-Merton Test Cases
Implied Volatility, Volatility Smiles and Volatility Surfaces

title: Implied Volatility, Volatility Smiles and Volatility Surfaces
text:
CPP Pricing Engine Alejandro Naranjo Caraza 2026-03-01

Overview
The following code extracts data on AAPL Put / Call option contracts and uses the option pricing engine to extract implied volatilities and plot volatility smiles and surfaces. Note that Put and Call contracts are kept seperate, as are Out-of-the-Money (OTM) and In-the-Money (ITM) contracts. Additionally, contracts are filtered based on the following criteria:

Only ITM or OTM contracts are considered (not both). ITM Calls satisfy: K < F. OTM Calls satisfy: K > F where K is the strike price and F is the underlying forward price.
Contracts with excessively large margins are excluded to avoid unrealistic prices.
Only contracts that satisfy no-arbitrage conditions are considered.

The no-arbitrage bounds for calls are 
\begin{aligned}
C_{\min} &= \max \big(F - K e^{-r T},\ 0 \big) &\quad& C_{\max} = F
\end{aligned}

Note that if a Call Option’s price is below its lower no-arbitrage bound 𝐶_{min}, an arbitrageur can short the underlying forward, buy the Call Option, and use the Call to cover the short position at maturity, thereby locking in a risk-free profit.
If a Call Option’s price exceeds its upper bound C_{max}, an arbitrageur can sell the Call Option and buy the underlying forward. At maturity, the Call’s payoff will be covered by the forward position, allowing the arbitrageur to lock in a risk-free profit.
while the no-arbitrage for puts are 
\begin{aligned}
P_{\min} &= \max \big(K e^{-r T} - F,\ 0 \big) &\quad& P_{\max} = K e^{-r T}
\end{aligned}

with F = S_0 e^{(r-q) T}
Note that if a Put Option’s price is below the lower bound P_{min}, an arbitrageur can buy the underlying forward and buy the Put Option. At maturity, the Put can be exercised to sell the underlying forward at the strike price, generating a risk-free profit.
If a Put Option’s price exceeds its upper bound P_{max}, an arbitrageur can sell the Put Option and sell the underlying forward. At maturity, the Put’s obligation will be offset by the forward position, resulting in a risk-free profit.


Library Import

import math
import sys
import datetime as dt

sys.path.append('../cpp_pricing/build')
import pricer
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import yfinance as yf



Import Options Data

aapl = yf.Ticker("AAPL")
expiries = list(aapl.options)
current_price = aapl.fast_info['lastPrice']


print('Expiry dates:')
for exp in expiries:
    print('\t',exp)
print('Current price:\t',round(current_price,2))

Expiry dates:
     2026-03-20
     2026-03-23
     2026-03-25
     2026-03-27
     2026-04-02
     2026-04-10
     2026-04-17
     2026-04-24
     2026-05-01
     2026-05-15
     2026-06-18
     2026-07-17
     2026-08-21
     2026-09-18
     2026-10-16
     2026-11-20
     2026-12-18
     2027-01-15
     2027-03-19
     2027-06-17
     2027-12-17
     2028-01-21
     2028-03-17
     2028-12-15
Current price:   248.96



today = dt.datetime.today().replace(hour=0, minute=0, second=0, microsecond=0)

S0 = current_price
r = 0.045
q = 0.005

calls_lst = []
puts_lst = []

for expiry in expiries:

    opt = aapl.option_chain(expiry)
    calls = opt.calls.copy()
    puts = opt.puts.copy()
    
    expiry_date = dt.datetime.strptime(expiry, "%Y-%m-%d")
    T = (expiry_date - today).days / 365.25

    if T<= 0:
        continue

    F = S0 * np.exp((r - q) * T)

    calls['T'] = T
    puts['T'] = T

    calls['F'] = F
    puts['F'] = F

    calls['moneyness'] = F / calls['strike']
    puts['moneyness'] = F / puts['strike']

    calls['logMoneyness'] = np.log(calls['moneyness'])
    puts['logMoneyness'] = np.log(puts['moneyness'])
    
    calls_lst.append(calls)
    puts_lst.append(puts)

all_calls = pd.concat(calls_lst, ignore_index=True)
all_puts = pd.concat(puts_lst, ignore_index=True)


all_calls.info()

Get Implied Volatility Through Pricing Engine

def getVol(data,optType):
    
    ivs = [] # Implied vol
    moneyness = [] # Moneyness (F/K)
    log_moneyness = [] # Log moneyness
    dtes = [] # Time to expiry
    sigma = 0.1 # Default volatility (does not matter in computations)
    eps = 1e-4 # Error term

    # Build stock object
    stock = pricer.makeStock(S0,q)

    # For each quotation, calculate implied volatility
    for idx, row in data.iterrows():

        # Filter contracts with negative bid, ask
        if row['bid'] <= 0 or row['ask'] <= 0:
            continue

        # Calculate price through mid between ask and bid
        mid = 0.5 * (row['bid'] + row['ask'])
        rel_spread = (row['ask'] - row['bid']) / mid
        P = mid
        K = row['strike']
        T = row['T']
        F = row['F']

        # Skip wide spreads (avoid unreasonable prices)
        if rel_spread > 0.2 or mid < 0.05:
            continue
            
        # Consider OTM only
        if optType == 'c' and K <= F:
            continue
    
        if optType == 'p' and K>=F:
            continue

        # Simple filter for outliers
        if abs(row['logMoneyness']) > 0.5:
            continue

        # Arbitrage-free bounds
        df_r = np.exp(-r * T)
        df_q = np.exp(-q * T)

        if optType == 'c':
            lower_bound = max(F - K * df_r, 0)      # intrinsic value
            upper_bound = F                         # max call price
        else:
            lower_bound = max(K * df_r - F, 0)      # intrinsic value
            upper_bound = K * df_r                   # max put price
        
        if P <= lower_bound + eps:
            continue
        if P >= upper_bound - eps:
            continue

        # Build vanilla option and BSM model and calculate implied volatility
        vanillaOption = pricer.makeVanillaOption(K,T,optType,'e')
        bsm = pricer.makeBSM(vanillaOption,stock,r,sigma)
        vol = bsm.impliedVol(P)
        
        if (np.isnan(vol)) or (vol <= 0) or (vol > 2):
            print("Warning. Incompatible vol found. Vol = ",vol)
            continue
            
        # Append data
        ivs.append(vol)
        moneyness.append(row['moneyness'])
        log_moneyness.append(row['logMoneyness'])
        dtes.append(T)
    return ivs, moneyness, log_moneyness, dtes



Call Option Volatility Smile

ivs, moneyness, log_moneyness, dtes = getVol(all_calls, 'c')

Warning. Incompatible vol found. Vol =  4.99999999996362
Warning. Incompatible vol found. Vol =  4.99999999996362
Warning. Incompatible vol found. Vol =  4.99999999996362



df = pd.DataFrame({"x": log_moneyness, "iv": ivs})
df = df.sort_values("x")
df["iv_smooth"] = df["iv"].rolling(30, center=True).mean()

plt.plot(df["x"], df["iv_smooth"])
plt.xlabel("log moneyness (K/S)")
plt.ylabel("Implied volatility")
plt.show()










Call Option Volatility Surface

%matplotlib inline  

fig, ax = plt.subplots(
    figsize=(8, 5),
    dpi=150,
    subplot_kw={"projection": "3d"}
)

surf = ax.plot_trisurf(
    moneyness,
    dtes,
    ivs,
    cmap="viridis",
    linewidth=0.2,
    antialiased=True,
    alpha=0.85
)

ax.set_xlabel("Moneyness (S/K)", labelpad=10)
ax.set_ylabel("Time to Expiration (T)", labelpad=10)
ax.set_zlabel("Implied Volatility", labelpad=10)

ax.view_init(elev=20, azim=-45)

fig.colorbar(surf, ax=ax, shrink=0.6, aspect=10, pad=0.1)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()










Put Option Volatility Smile

ivs, moneyness, log_moneyness, dtes = getVol(all_puts, 'p')

Warning. Incompatible vol found. Vol =  4.99999999996362



df = pd.DataFrame({"x": log_moneyness, "iv": ivs})
df = df.sort_values("x")

plt.plot(df["x"], df["iv_smooth"])
plt.xlabel("log moneyness (K/S)")
plt.ylabel("Implied volatility")
plt.show()










Put Option Volatility Surface

%matplotlib inline  

fig, ax = plt.subplots(
    figsize=(8, 5),
    dpi=150,
    subplot_kw={"projection": "3d"}
)

surf = ax.plot_trisurf(
    moneyness,
    dtes,
    ivs,
    cmap="viridis",
    linewidth=0.2,
    antialiased=True,
    alpha=0.85
)

ax.set_xlabel("Moneyness (S/K)", labelpad=10)
ax.set_ylabel("Time to Expiration (T)", labelpad=10)
ax.set_zlabel("Implied Volatility", labelpad=10)

ax.view_init(elev=20, azim=20)

fig.colorbar(surf, ax=ax, shrink=0.6, aspect=10, pad=0.1)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Binomial Tree and Black-Scholes-Merton Overview and Implementation
text:
CPP Pricing Engine Alejandro Naranjo Caraza 2026-03-01

Overview
